era_context: The Roman Empire under the Tetrarchy system established by Diocletian (284–305 CE) is fragmenting. Maxentius rules Italy and North Africa; Constantine I controls Britain, Gaul, and Hispania; Licinius and Maximinus Daia contest the Eastern provinces. The Great Persecution of Christians (303–311 CE) continues under Galerius and Maximinus. In China, the Western Jin Dynasty is collapsing into the War of the Eight Princes (291–306 CE), with the empire fragmenting into competing warlord states. This is a period of acute political instability across the Mediterranean and East Asia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and Silk Road trade dynamics

events.title: Constantine I's Vision at the Bridge of Milvian (traditional dating; actual battle 312 CE)
events.description: Constantine I reports a vision of a cross in the sky, which he interprets as a divine sign to adopt Christian symbols. This event is traditionally dated to 310 CE by some sources, though the famous Battle of the Milvian Bridge where Constantine defeated Maxentius occurred in 312 CE. The vision narrative is preserved in Eusebius of Caesarea's Life of Constantine, written after Constantine's death. Whether the vision occurred in 310 or 312, or was a later hagiographic invention, remains debated. This marks the beginning of Constantine's association with Christianity, which would transform the religion's status within the empire.
key_figures: Eusebius of Caesarea (Christian historian, c. 260–340 CE)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine (Vita Constantini), Book 1, chapters 27–32
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors (De mortibus persecutorum), chapter 44

events.certainty_note: The vision is attested only in post-Constantinian sources written decades later. No contemporary documentation exists. The date itself is uncertain—some sources place it at the Milvian Bridge (312 CE). The event may be hagiographic rather than historical.

events.title: Maxentius Proclaimed Emperor in Rome
events.description: Maxentius, son of the retired emperor Maximian, is proclaimed emperor by the Roman Senate and praetorian guard, establishing control over Italy, North Africa, and Sicily. He rules in opposition to Constantine I (who controls Gaul and Britain) and the Eastern emperors Licinius and Maximinus Daia. Maxentius's reign is marked by attempts to restore traditional pagan religion and Roman civic institutions, positioning himself as the defender of Rome against the Tetrarchic system. His rule lasts until his defeat and death at the Milvian Bridge in 312 CE.
key_figures: Maximian (retired emperor, father of Maxentius, r. 286–305 CE)

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, Life of Constantine, Book 1, chapters 24–26
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 40–43
Roman coins and inscriptions from the reign of Maxentius

events.certainty_note: Maxentius's proclamation and reign are well-attested in numismatic evidence, inscriptions, and multiple historical chronicles. The date 306 CE (or possibly 307 CE) is secure.

events.title: Galerius Issues Edict of Toleration for Christians
events.description: Galerius, the senior Eastern emperor, issues an edict granting toleration to Christians and ending the Great Persecution (303–311 CE). The edict is issued from Nicomedia in Bithynia, likely in late 310 or early 311 CE. Galerius had been the primary architect of the persecution under Diocletian; his reversal is attributed to illness (possibly gout or a wasting disease) and pragmatic recognition that persecution had failed to eliminate Christianity. The edict permits Christians to worship openly but does not restore confiscated property. This marks a turning point in Christian-imperial relations, though persecution continues under Maximinus Daia in the Eastern provinces.

sources.name: Eusebius of Caesarea, History of the Church (Ecclesiastical History), Book 8, chapters 16–17
Lactantius, On the Deaths of the Persecutors, chapters 33–35

events.certainty_note: The edict is attested in multiple near-contemporary sources, but the exact date is uncertain—sources place it between late 310 and early 311 CE. The text of the edict itself does not survive; we know it only through paraphrases in Eusebius and Lactantius.

events.title: Western Jin Dynasty Collapse Accelerates; Warlord Fragmentation in China
events.description: The Western Jin Dynasty (266–316 CE), already weakened by the War of the Eight Princes (291–306 CE), continues to fragment as regional warlords consolidate power. By 310 CE, the central government's authority is nominal; competing military strongmen control provinces. This period sees the beginning of large-scale migration of non-Han peoples (Xiongnu, Xianbei, Jie, Di, Qiang) into North China, which will lead to the establishment of the Sixteen Kingdoms (304–439 CE). The south remains under nominal Jin control but is increasingly autonomous. This represents one of the most chaotic periods in Chinese history, with widespread warfare, famine, and displacement.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) and Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government), compiled by Sima Guang (11th century CE)
Book of Jin (Jin Shu), compiled in the 7th century CE
Archaeological evidence from tomb inscriptions and ceramic remains

events.certainty_note: The general trajectory of Jin collapse is well-established, but precise dating of individual events within 310 CE is difficult. Chinese historical sources are detailed but were compiled centuries later and may contain legendary elements.

events.title: Shapur II Consolidates Sassanid Control in Persia
events.description: Shapur II (r. 309–379 CE), known as 'Shapur of the Shoulder' (Shapur Dhū'l-Aktāf), begins his long reign as Sassanid Shah. He is crowned while still in the womb according to tradition, and by 310 CE has consolidated power after a period of internal instability. Shapur II will become one of the greatest Sassanid rulers, known for military campaigns against Rome, the conquest of Ctesiphon, and religious reforms. His reign marks the height of Sassanid power and represents a counterbalance to Constantine I's rise in the West.

sources.name: Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk), compiled 9th century CE
Ammianus Marcellinus, Roman History, Books 14–31 (4th century CE)

events.certainty_note: Shapur II's accession and reign are well-attested in Sassanid numismatic evidence and later Persian chronicles. The date 309 CE is secure.
cross_references: 337-001 (Constantine I dies; Shapur II campaigns against Rome)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Constantine's vision is highly disputed. While traditionally placed in 310 CE, many modern scholars argue it was a later hagiographic invention or occurred at the Milvian Bridge in 312 CE. Eusebius's account, written c. 337–340 CE, is the only source and was composed after Constantine's death with clear propagandistic intent. The vision narrative may reflect Constantine's later Christian piety rather than a historical event. Additionally, the extent of Maxentius's 'paganism' versus Constantine's 'Christianity' is often overstated in older historiography; both rulers were pragmatic about religious policy, and the religious framing of their conflict is partly a later Christian narrative.
historiographic_note: The year 310 CE is moderately well-documented for the Roman Empire through numismatic evidence, inscriptions, and near-contemporary chronicles (Eusebius, Lactantius), though these sources were written 20–30 years after events and carry Christian apologetic bias. For China, sources are detailed but compiled centuries later (7th–11th centuries CE) and may conflate or misdate events. For Sassanid Persia, evidence is primarily numismatic and from much later Persian chronicles. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are essentially absent from surviving written sources for this year. The historical record is heavily weighted toward Mediterranean and East Asian elites.

graph_edges.note: Constantine's vision (or its later invention) is presented as motivating his victory at Milvian Bridge
Maxentius's rule directly precipitated the conflict with Constantine
Galerius's edict of toleration paved the way for the Edict of Milan
Continued fragmentation of Western Jin led to its final collapse
Shapur II's rise paralleled Constantine I's consolidation of power in the West
